exemplar.title: To what extent is Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing a valid form of psychological treatment for post traumatic stress disorder?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing a valid form of psychological treatment for post traumatic stress disorder?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clearly stated and evaluative research question
Logical use of headings and contents page to guide the reader
Introduction outlines an evaluative purpose and key treatments
Topic of EMDR for PTSD communicated adequately
weaknesses: Focus drifts into general prevalence and COVID-19 statistics
Research question remains broad and âvalidityâ is not operationalised
Methodology lacks clear selection criteria and rationale
First-person language in introduction reduces academic tone
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments indicate an adequate but only partially sustained focus (band 3â4), a clear but broad research question (band 3â4) and a limited, unsystematic methodology (band 1â2). On balance, the work meets the descriptors for the mid-band of Focus and Method, meriting 3 marks.

strengths: Sound general knowledge of PTSD and EMDR with reference to RCTs and AIP theory
Mostly accurate use of subject-specific terminology (e.g., bilateral stimulation, relapse)
Coherent historical account of EMDRâs origin
Use of authoritative sources such as NHS definitions
weaknesses: Reliance on popular websites and anecdotal case material limits depth
Inconsistent or missing citations for key statistics and dates
Occasional imprecise descriptions (e.g., âspeech therapy with side-to-side eye motionsâ)
Conflicting prevalence figures reduce data credibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good knowledge and mostly accurate terminology (band 3â4), but inconsistencies in source use, occasional inaccuracies and reliance on non-scholarly material prevent an excellent rating. A score of 3 marks reflects this solid yet uneven performance.

strengths: Use of multiple journal articles and official guidelines to support arguments
Insightful comparison between EMDR and other therapies (CBT, antidepressants)
Some supported analytical passages with cited statistics
Recognition of methodological limitations (e.g., RCT weaknesses)
weaknesses: Analysis often remains descriptive rather than fully evaluative
Source credibility is not consistently appraised (blogs, commercial sites)
Criteria for âvalidityâ are implied rather than explicitly applied
Critical evaluation of biases and ethical issues is uneven and underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical Thinking strands fall solidly in the 4â6 band: research is mostly relevant, analysis is clear but partially supported, and the argument shows reasoned evaluation without full critical depth. The performance warrants 4 marks.

strengths: Complete structure with title page, contents, introduction, conclusion, bibliography and appendices
Detailed and logical contents page enhances navigation
Headings and sectioning follow EE conventions
weaknesses: Inconsistent formatting (LaTeX math-mode page numbers, mixed citation styles)
Some heading styles and font sizes are not uniform
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Presentation criterion shows a clearly appropriate structure (band 3â4) tempered by inconsistent formatting (band 1â2). This mixed performance aligns with the mid-band, justifying 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychology terminology (PTSD, EMDR phases, bilateral stimulation, RCT, relapse) is generally used correctly and consistently.  Occasional inaccuracies (âFMDRâ, âEve Movementâ) and informal phrasing reduce precision, but overall usage is mostly accurate, meriting the middle band.

Basic layout requirements are met: word count, title, pagination and a bibliography are provided.  However, inconsistent formatting (math-mode page numbers, mixed citation styles, occasional spacing errors) shows only partial adherence to IB presentation guidelines, warranting the 1-2 band.

The student demonstrates sound general knowledge of PTSD and EMDR, referencing RCTs, AIP theory and comparative therapies.  Psychological concepts such as bilateral stimulation and trauma-focused CBT are used appropriately, and citations are provided.  Nevertheless, reliance on popular websites and anecdotal case material limits depth, so knowledge is good but not consistently excellent.

Methodology is limited to âusing the internet, Google Scholar and websitesâ.  There is no rationale for source selection, no inclusion/exclusion criteria and no discussion of psychological research methods.  Although a range of web articles, blogs and a few journal papers is cited, evidence of informed or systematic selection is minimal, placing the work in the lower band for methodology.

The student states a clear topic â the validity of EMDR for PTSD â and outlines a basic purpose (to examine effectiveness, compare with alternatives and judge validity).  Headings and a contents page help communicate scope.  Nevertheless, the explanation of focus drifts into prevalence statistics, COVID-19 and general definitions, so the intended line of inquiry is only partially maintained.  Overall, the topic is communicated adequately but not with the sustained precision expected of the top band.

The essay draws on a fair number of sources, including journal articles and official guidelines, which are for the most part relevant to the question.  However, quality is mixed (blogs, commercial websites) and source credibility is not critically appraised.  Relevance to the research question is usually clear but not always sustained, justifying the lower end of the 4-6 band.

The research question is explicitly presented on the title page and is phrased as an evaluative question.  However, it remains broad (-âto what extentâ¦ valid form of treatmentâ-) and the essay does not operationalise âvalidâ (efficacy, cost, ethics, etc.) in a tightly-focused manner.  Connections to subsequent discussion are present but only partly sustained, showing a moderate rather than strong focus.

The essay follows EE conventions with title page, contents, headings, introduction, conclusion, bibliography and appendices.  Logical sectioning aids readability, though some headings are mis-numbered and page indicators (e.g., maths fonts) are unconventional.  Overall the structure is appropriate and complete, aligning with the 3-4 band.

The discussion highlights strengths and limitations of EMDR, considers cost, side-effects and ethical issues, and ends with a reasoned conclusion.  Critical evaluation is present but uneven; for example, issues of placebo or publication bias are noted but not explored in depth, and criteria for judging âvalidityâ remain implicit.  Argument structure is clear but not fully rigorous, so the work fits the 4-6 descriptor.

Analytical passages compare EMDR with antidepressants, CBT and CPT, and note methodological limits of RCTs.  Some claims are supported by cited statistics (e.g., relapse rate 56%).  Yet analysis often remains descriptive, with limited synthesis of findings and no clear evaluative framework.  Conclusions are partly supported, placing analysis at the threshold of the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: The student has clearly presented the title and research question at the start, fulfilling IB formatting requirements.
The word count is provided and meets the 4,000-word limit, demonstrating attention to formatting guidelines.
While the research question is explicit, operationalise âvalidâ with specific criteria (e.g., efficacy, cost, ethics) to sharpen focus.
The contents page is detailed and logically structured, aiding navigation through the essayâs sections.
Page numbers appear in LaTeX math mode, which deviates from IB conventions; use regular numerals instead.
Standardise heading styles and font sizes in the table of contents to ensure consistent presentation.
The introduction uses first-person language (âI am going to exploreâ), which is informal; adopt an objective academic tone.
Methodology lacks clear selection criteria and rationale for sources, limiting the systematic rigour of the research method.
The student outlines an evaluative purposeâto assess EMDRâs positives and negatives and compare with other treatmentsâshowing clear intent.
The discussion of COVID-19 and general PTSD prevalence shifts away from EMDR validity, diluting the essayâs central focus.
When presenting AIP, insert an in-text citation (e.g., Hill, 2020) at first mention to improve source clarity.
Contrasting EMDRâs memory-based approach with cognitive therapies reflects insightful analysis and comparison.
Description of EMDR as âspeech therapy with side-to-side eye motionsâ is imprecise; refine to accurately reflect the eight-phase protocol.
Cite recognised guidelines (e.g., APA, NICE) when stating EMDRâs recommended status to bolster the argument with authoritative support.
The historical account of EMDRâs origin and development is coherent and clearly presented.
The introduction year â1989â is stated without citation; reference Shapiroâs original publication to validate this date.
Consider summarising the eight phases of EMDR here or cross-referencing Appendix 1 to guide readers through the protocol.
Incorporating Zhang et al. (2021) on increased PTSD prevalence effectively contextualises the investigationâs significance.
Clarify how the pandemic specifically influenced EMDR research rather than general PTSD statistics to maintain relevance.
Clear definition of PTSD from a credible NHS source demonstrates solid subject knowledge and use of authoritative references.
The statistic â1 in 3 people experiencing itâ conflicts with the later 6% figure; verify prevalence data for consistency.
Provide precise references for all statistics and specify whether prevalence refers to lifetime or point estimates.
Introduction to key PTSD treatments and signposting of EMDR focus is clear and well-structured.
Assertion that âantidepressants are relatively popularâ requires citation and supporting data to uphold credibility.
Define and justify inclusion/exclusion criteria for treatments compared to EMDR to strengthen methodological rigour.
Typographical error in heading âWhat is FMDR?â undermines professionalism; correct to âEMDRâ.
Discussion of Adaptive Information Processing (AIP) theory demonstrates strong conceptual understanding of EMDRâs foundations.
